stem: Evaluate the improper integral \( \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{[[n]]} e^{-[[a]]x}\,dx \).
solution: This is a Gamma-function integral: \( \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{[[n]]} e^{-[[a]]x}\,dx = \tfrac{\Gamma([[n]]+1)}{[[a]]^{[[n]]+1}} = \tfrac{[[n]]!}{[[a]]^{[[n]]+1}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives \( [[n]]! \) but forgets to divide by \( [[a]]^{[[n]]+1} \).
This uses \( ([[n]]+1)! \), i.e. \( \Gamma([[n]]+2) \), the wrong Gamma value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This halves the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the Gaussian-type improper integral \( \int_{0}^{\infty} [[c]]x^{[[n]]} e^{-[[a]]x^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: Substitute \( u = x^{2} \) (so \( du = 2x\,dx \)) to reduce this to a Gamma-function integral: with \( [[n]] = 2k+1 \), \( \int_{0}^{\infty} [[c]]x^{[[n]]}e^{-[[a]]x^{2}}\,dx = \tfrac{[[c]]}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{k}e^{-[[a]]u}\,du = \tfrac{[[c]]\,k!}{2\,[[a]]^{k+1}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} \) instead of the correctly reduced power of \( [[a]] \) after the \( u=x^{2} \) substitution.
This handles only the \( n=1 \) case's structure regardless of the actual exponent \( [[n]] \).
This forgets the factor of \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) that comes from \( du = 2x\,dx \).
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This halves the correct value.

stem: The Gamma function satisfies \( \Gamma(n) = (n-1)! \) for positive integers \( n \). Using this relation (with no integration needed), evaluate \( [[k]]\,\Gamma([[n]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \Gamma(n) = n! \), off by one in the factorial relation.
This uses \( \Gamma(n) = (n+1)! \), off by two in the factorial relation.
This drops the leading coefficient \( [[k]] \) entirely.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This halves the correct value.

stem: Using the Beta-function relation \( \int_{0}^{1} x^{m}(1-x)^{n}\,dx = \dfrac{m!\,n!}{(m+n+1)!} \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{1} [[c]]x^{[[m]]}(1-x)^{[[n]]}\,dx \).
solution: By the Beta-function identity, \( \int_{0}^{1} x^{[[m]]}(1-x)^{[[n]]}\,dx = \dfrac{[[m]]!\,[[n]]!}{([[m]]+[[n]]+1)!} \), so the integral equals \( [[c]]\cdot\dfrac{[[m]]!\,[[n]]!}{([[m]]+[[n]]+1)!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( (m+n)! \) in the denominator instead of \( (m+n+1)! \), off by one in the factorial.
This replaces the Beta identity with a rough guess based on the exponents plus one.
This inverts the correct Beta-function ratio.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
